does: This paper addresses the challenge of auxiliary line generation in geometry reasoning for vision-language models by proposing a reinforcement learning framework that represents auxiliary lines as structured textual descriptions rather than code-rendered visuals. The core innovation is a cross-modal reward model that evaluates alignment between generated textual descriptions of auxiliary lines and ground-truth geometric diagrams. The authors use this reward signal to train an LLM via GRPO-based reinforcement learning, enabling the model to generate informative auxiliary-line descriptions that improve downstream geometric reasoning. The approach prioritizes robustness and simplicity over the brittleness of code generation in complex solid geometry settings.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to enhance LVLMs for mathematical reasoning, particularly geometry problem-solving where auxiliary constructions are critical but underexplored. It responds to limitations of prior code-driven rendering approaches that struggle with precision requirements in complex 3D geometry. The paper extends research in cross-modal learning and diagram understanding in vision-language models, leveraging advances in reinforcement learning for language models like GRPO. It also connects to broader work on multimodal reward models and diagram-text alignment, drawing on recent progress in using RL to improve mathematical reasoning in LLMs and the importance of auxiliary information for solving geometric problems.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more robust geometric reasoning in vision-language models by decoupling auxiliary construction from fragile code generation. The cross-modal reward model approach could generalize to other geometric domains and mathematical fields requiring spatial reasoning with textual explanation. The work enables future research into hybrid reasoning systems that combine visual and linguistic modalities for mathematical problem-solving, and suggests that structured textual descriptions may be more scalable than code generation for complex geometric constructions. It also points toward improved multimodal reward modeling and RL training methods for mathematical reasoning, with potential applications to educational systems, automated tutoring, and diagram understanding in general.
